entries.text: Many people have begun reassessing their relationship with digital technology in recent years. The constant barrage of notifications, the pressure to maintain an online presence, and the erosion of uninterrupted leisure time have prompted a growing number of individuals to disconnect deliberately. What began as a niche practice has evolved into a widespread movement known as digital minimalism. Adherents report improved concentration, deeper interpersonal connections, and a renewed sense of agency over their daily lives.

You know, a lot of people have started to really question their relationship with technology. And honestly, I think the reason is pretty clear — the constant notifications, the pressure to always be online, the way your free time just gets eaten up. So more and more people are choosing to disconnect — on purpose. It started as this niche thing, but now it's become this whole movement — they call it digital minimalism. And the people who try it say their concentration gets better, their relationships feel deeper, and they actually feel like they're in control of their own lives again.

Grammatical range and accuracy are among the most heavily weighted criteria in the IELTS Writing and Speaking tests. Examiners are not merely looking for error-free sentences; they are assessing whether the candidate can manipulate complex structures with flexibility and precision. A response that relies predominantly on simple and compound sentences, however accurate, will struggle to exceed Band 6.5. By contrast, the skilful use of subordinate clauses, conditional structures, and participial phrases can elevate a performance from adequate to accomplished.

When it comes to IELTS, your grammatical range and accuracy matter a lot. It's one of the most heavily weighted criteria in both Writing and Speaking. And here's the thing — examiners are not just checking if your sentences are error-free. They want to see if you can actually use complex structures flexibly and precisely. If your response is mostly simple and compound sentences, even if they're correct, you'll struggle to get above a 6.5. On the other hand, if you can use subordinate clauses, conditionals, and participle clauses skilfully — that's what pushes you from adequate to accomplished.
So the tech I decided to step back from — well, it was Instagram.

I'd been using it for about three years, and honestly, I didn't even realise how much it was affecting me until one evening I noticed I'd spent nearly three hours just scrolling — and I felt worse than before I'd started.
The thing was, everyone's life looked so perfectly curated. I kept comparing — you know, my everyday reality to these idealised pictures. And that was doing a lot of damage, looking back.
So I deleted the app. Just as an experiment — for a week. And the effect was pretty immediate.
I had so much more time. I started reading again, exercising, having proper conversations.
That low-level anxiety I hadn't even fully acknowledged — it just started to fade.
Would I recommend it? Honestly, yes — if you feel like technology is taking over, try it.
But I'm not saying go completely off-grid. Just be more intentional about how you use it. That's really the key.
Honestly? I'm on my phone pretty much all day.
What gets me is how much our relationship with technology has changed — and how fast.
So I deleted Instagram. Just like that.
It actually makes a real difference to your wellbeing.
OK so the tech I decided to ditch was my smartphone.
What happened was I'd been spending like four hours a day on social media.
The thing that surprised me most was how quickly my concentration improved.
So yeah I'd definitely tell other people to give it a try.
I think companies absolutely have a responsibility here. I mean, these platforms are designed to keep you scrolling — that's not an accident.
Smartphones have completely changed how we talk to each other — and honestly, not always for the better.
There's some truth to that. If you're working multiple jobs or rely on your phone for income, switching off isn't really an option.
I think we're only just starting to understand the long-term effects — and some of the early signs are worrying.
In my opinion, yes — I think some level of regulation is not just desirable but necessary.
Autonomous vehicles, which rely on artificial intelligence, are becoming safer.
Although there are concerns about job losses, the benefits outweigh the drawbacks.
If emissions continue to rise, global temperatures will increase significantly.
If the government had invested more in education, we wouldn't be facing this skills shortage now.
Advancing rapidly, technology will automate many jobs.
If the government had regulated social media, young people wouldn't be at risk now.
Countries that invest heavily in renewable energy are seeing economic benefits.
Although artificial intelligence has great potential, it also poses significant ethical risks.
